questions.question: What was the significance of the Congress of Vienna (1814-1815) after Napoleon's defeat?
options: The Congress of Vienna created the League of Nations and established international arbitration for disputes between nations

The Congress of Vienna redrew the map of Europe after Napoleon, establishing a 'Concert of Europe' — a balance-of-power system among major powers (Austria, Prussia, Russia, Britain, France) that largely prevented major European wars for 99 years until WWI. Reactionary in character, it restored monarchies Napoleon had overthrown
The Congress of Vienna established democratic constitutions in France, Spain, and Italy, spreading Enlightenment principles across Europe after Napoleon's revolution
The Congress of Vienna divided Napoleon's empire among Britain, Austria, and Russia as colonial territories
questions.answer: The Congress of Vienna redrew the map of Europe after Napoleon, establishing a 'Concert of Europe' — a balance-of-power system among major powers (Austria, Prussia, Russia, Britain, France) that largely prevented major European wars for 99 years until WWI. Reactionary in character, it restored monarchies Napoleon had overthrown
questions.explanation: The Congress of Vienna (September 1814 – June 1815), masterminded by Austrian diplomat Klemens von Metternich, was deliberately conservative: restore the pre-revolutionary order, suppress nationalism and liberalism, prevent any single power from dominating Europe. The 'Concert of Europe' system of great-power consultation lasted until 1914. The key achievement: 99 years without a major pan-European war (there were smaller wars — Crimean War, Franco-Prussian War — but no continent-wide conflict). Compared to the Versailles Treaty outcome (another world war in 21 years), Vienna was extraordinarily successful.
questions.memory_hook: The Congress of Vienna was designed to prevent another Napoleon — and it mostly worked for 99 years. Metternich's insight: a balance of power requires that no one feel humiliated enough to overturn the system. Compare to Versailles (1919): the victors humiliated Germany, which produced Hitler in 14 years. Vienna's settlement lasted a century; Versailles lasted 20 years. How you treat the defeated matters.

questions.question: The Vietnam War ended in 1975 with the fall of Saigon. What was the immediate cause of the US withdrawing from South Vietnam?
options: The US Congress cut all military funding to South Vietnam after the Watergate scandal discredited President Nixon's credibility, making continued war politically untenable

The Paris Peace Accords (1973) ended direct US military involvement; the US continued funding South Vietnam until Congress cut aid in 1974-75. North Vietnam's final offensive began in January 1975 and overran South Vietnam faster than anyone predicted — Saigon fell April 30, 1975
American military defeats in the Tet Offensive (1968) forced an immediate withdrawal from South Vietnam within three months
President Ford authorized a final military strike that failed, leading Congress to pass a resolution formally ending the war
questions.answer: The Paris Peace Accords (1973) ended direct US military involvement; the US continued funding South Vietnam until Congress cut aid in 1974-75. North Vietnam's final offensive began in January 1975 and overran South Vietnam faster than anyone predicted — Saigon fell April 30, 1975
questions.explanation: The Paris Peace Accords (January 1973) were signed under Nixon: the US withdrew troops; North Vietnam agreed to a ceasefire. Both sides violated it immediately. The Watergate scandal (1973-74) weakened Nixon's ability to enforce US commitments; he resigned in August 1974. Congress cut military aid to South Vietnam in 1974. In January 1975, North Vietnam launched a final offensive; the ARVN (South Vietnamese army) collapsed faster than US military advisors predicted. Saigon fell April 30, 1975 — the helicopter evacuation from the US Embassy roof became one of history's most iconic images. Approximately 58,000 US soldiers and 2-3.5 million Vietnamese died in the war.
questions.memory_hook: The US helicopter evacuation from the Saigon embassy roof on April 30, 1975 — soldiers cutting the line as desperate Vietnamese tried to board — is one of history's most photographed moments of American failure. Nixon had promised South Vietnam continued US support; Congress cut the money. South Vietnam fell in weeks. The war killed 58,000 Americans and at least 2 million Vietnamese, ending with the side the US backed surrendering unconditionally.

questions.question: What was the Rwandan Genocide (1994) and how many people were killed?
options: The Rwandan Genocide was a decade-long civil war between Hutu and Tutsi ethnic groups that killed approximately 50,000 soldiers on both sides

In approximately 100 days (April-July 1994), an estimated 500,000-800,000 Rwandan Tutsi and moderate Hutu were killed by Hutu extremists using machetes and firearms — while the UN and Western powers refused to intervene despite clear warning signs
The Rwandan Genocide was carried out by Belgian colonizers who returned to Rwanda in 1994 to suppress a pro-independence movement
A drought caused widespread famine in Rwanda in 1994, which international media called a 'genocide' for political reasons; the death toll was approximately 20,000
questions.answer: In approximately 100 days (April-July 1994), an estimated 500,000-800,000 Rwandan Tutsi and moderate Hutu were killed by Hutu extremists using machetes and firearms — while the UN and Western powers refused to intervene despite clear warning signs
questions.explanation: The Rwandan Genocide began April 6, 1994 after President Habyarimana's plane was shot down. Radio stations (RTLM) broadcast instructions and lists of targets; neighbors killed neighbors. The UN peacekeeping commander (General Roméo Dallaire) sent the 'genocide fax' to New York weeks before, warning of planned massacres and requesting authorization to act — he was refused. The US deliberately avoided using the word 'genocide' to avoid legal obligations to intervene. The genocide killed approximately 75% of Rwanda's Tutsi population in 100 days — a rate of murder faster than the Holocaust. The Rwandan Patriotic Front (Tutsi exile army) stopped the genocide by military victory.
questions.memory_hook: General Dallaire sent a fax to the UN warning of planned genocide six weeks before it started — including details of weapon caches and kill lists. The UN told him to do nothing. The US State Department was ordered to avoid saying the word 'genocide' because it would have triggered legal obligations to act. 800,000 people died in 100 days. Rwanda's genocide rate exceeded the Holocaust's pace. Dallaire later said he could have stopped it with 5,000 troops. He was given none.

questions.question: What is the 'Great Eight' in swimming and which swimmer accomplished it?
options: The 'Great Eight' is a mythological term for any swimmer who wins 8 consecutive Olympic relay gold medals — no swimmer has accomplished this

Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics — the most gold medals won by any athlete in a single Olympics. He won the 100m butterfly by 0.01 seconds, the closest finish in Olympic swimming history, contributing to the '8 for 8'
Mark Spitz won the 'Great Eight' at the 1972 Munich Olympics, setting world records in all 7 events he entered
Ian Thorpe won 8 medals at the 2000 Sydney Olympics, including 5 gold, the most by any Australian Olympian
questions.answer: Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics — the most gold medals won by any athlete in a single Olympics. He won the 100m butterfly by 0.01 seconds, the closest finish in Olympic swimming history, contributing to the '8 for 8'
questions.explanation: Michael Phelps's 2008 Beijing performance: 8 gold medals, 7 world records. The 100m butterfly finish against Milorad Cavic was decided by 0.01 seconds — a photo finish so close that Phelps's touch pad registered first only because he stretched his last stroke while Cavic glided. Phelps's career total: 28 Olympic medals (23 gold, 3 silver, 2 bronze) — the most decorated Olympian in history. His wingspan (198 cm) exceeded his height (193 cm), his torso was proportionally long, and his legs shorter — advantages in swimming geometry. He began competitive swimming at age 7 largely to channel hyperactivity (he was diagnosed with ADHD).
questions.memory_hook: Phelps won his 7th gold in 2008 by 0.01 seconds — Cavic had already stopped stroking because he thought he'd won. Phelps took one more half-stroke and touched the pad a hundredth of a second earlier. The 8th gold that nobody thought was possible came from that half-stroke. Phelps was diagnosed with ADHD at age 7; his coach suggested swimming as an outlet.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Masters Tournament and Augusta National Golf Club?
options: The Masters is the oldest golf major, played at Augusta National since 1934 — a private club that did not admit Black members until 1990 or female members until 2012. The winner receives the iconic green jacket, placed on the champion by the previous year's winner
The Masters is played at different locations each year, similar to the British Open, rotating among Augusta, Pebble Beach, and St. Andrews
Augusta National has hosted the Masters since 1934 and has always had diverse, inclusive membership policies that reflect American democratic values
The Masters is the only golf major played outside the United States, at Augusta, Georgia — a city in the UK
questions.answer: The Masters is the oldest golf major, played at Augusta National since 1934 — a private club that did not admit Black members until 1990 or female members until 2012. The winner receives the iconic green jacket, placed on the champion by the previous year's winner
questions.explanation: Augusta National Golf Club was co-founded by Bobby Jones and opened in 1933. The Masters began in 1934. The green jacket tradition began in 1949. Controversies: the club excluded Black members until Ron Townsend was admitted in 1990; women were excluded until 2012 (when Condoleezza Rice and Darla Moore became the first female members, after pressure from a campaign by the National Council of Women's Organizations). Tiger Woods won the Masters in 1997 (first Black champion), 2001, 2002, 2005, and 2019 (comeback after back surgery and personal scandals). The course features holes named after plants and trees.
questions.memory_hook: Augusta National didn't admit a Black member until 1990 — 56 years after the first Masters was played. Tiger Woods won there in 1997. The club didn't admit women until 2012, after national-level public pressure campaigns. The green jacket is lent, not owned: winners must return it to the clubhouse and can only take it out for limited public appearances. It's the most exclusive garment in sports.

questions.question: Which Japanese animated studio created 'Princess Mononoke' (1997) and 'Howl's Moving Castle' (2004)?
options: Toei Animation
Studio 4°C
Madhouse
Studio Ghibli
questions.explanation: Studio Ghibli was founded in 1985 by Hayao Miyazaki, Isao Takahata, and Toshio Suzuki. Key films: My Neighbor Totoro (1988), Grave of the Fireflies (1988), Kiki's Delivery Service (1989), Princess Mononoke (1997), Spirited Away (2001 — Oscar for Best Animated Feature), Howl's Moving Castle (2004), Ponyo (2008), The Wind Rises (2013). Ghibli's films are notable for hand-drawn animation (largely rejecting CGI), complex female protagonists, environmental themes, and lack of clear villains. Miyazaki has 'retired' multiple times; the studio continues under new directors including his son Goro Miyazaki.
questions.memory_hook: Studio Ghibli's Spirited Away has earned more money in Japan than any film in history, including Titanic and Star Wars. Ghibli's approach — complex female protagonists, no clear villains, environmental themes, hand-drawn animation — is explicitly a rejection of what Miyazaki sees as American animation's formulas. He has said Disney films are based on the assumption that audiences are stupid; Ghibli films assume they are not.

questions.question: Which playwright wrote 'Death of a Salesman' (1949), featuring Willy Loman and the dark side of the American Dream?
options: Tennessee Williams
Eugene O'Neill
Edward Albee
Arthur Miller
questions.explanation: Arthur Miller's Death of a Salesman (1949) premiered on Broadway and won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama. Willy Loman (a salesman who has spent his life chasing success and recognition, failing, and deluding himself) has become the defining literary character of American disillusionment with the 'success myth.' Miller said he intentionally named the character 'Low-Man.' The play premiered with Lee J. Cobb as Willy; later productions starred Dustin Hoffman and Philip Seymour Hoffman. Miller married Marilyn Monroe in 1956 and wrote The Crucible (1953) as an allegory of McCarthyism.
questions.memory_hook: Arthur Miller wrote The Crucible (Salem witch trials) as a direct allegory for McCarthyism while being investigated by McCarthy's committee himself. He refused to name names and was convicted of contempt of Congress (overturned on appeal). His wife at the time was Marilyn Monroe. Death of a Salesman premiered the same year as 1984 — 1949 was the year American literature asked: is the American Dream real, or is it a trap?

questions.question: Which 1984 novel by Umberto Eco is set in a medieval Italian monastery where a series of murders surround a lost book by Aristotle?
options: The Prague Cemetery
Foucault's Pendulum
The Island of the Day Before
The Name of the Rose
questions.explanation: Umberto Eco's The Name of the Rose (1980 in Italian; English 1983) follows Franciscan friar William of Baskerville (named as a tribute to Sherlock Holmes) investigating murders in a 14th-century monastery. The lost book is Aristotle's second book of the Poetics (on comedy) — the murderer believes laughter undermines the fear of God and kills to protect the text. The novel blends medieval history, semiotics (Eco was a semiotics professor), and detective fiction. It was a surprise bestseller — an academic novel became one of Italy's best-selling books. The 1986 film starred Sean Connery.
questions.memory_hook: The Name of the Rose is a murder mystery set in a medieval monastery built around a lost book by Aristotle on comedy. The killer believes comedy is dangerous — that if people laugh at authority, they stop fearing it, and if they stop fearing God, civilization collapses. Umberto Eco was a semiotics professor who wrote a bestselling thriller. Sean Connery played the detective monk. It's one of the most unlikely bestsellers in publishing history.

questions.question: The Caspian Sea is classified as a lake, not a sea. How large is it and which countries border it?
options: The Caspian Sea is about the size of Lake Superior; it borders Russia, Kazakhstan, and Azerbaijan

The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area (~371,000 km²) and borders five countries: Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan. It contains large reserves of oil and natural gas under its bed
The Caspian Sea borders Turkey, Iran, Armenia, Georgia, and Russia; its classification as a sea means it falls under maritime law
The Caspian Sea is a freshwater lake shared between Iran and Russia that has been shrinking due to water diversion for agriculture
questions.answer: The Caspian Sea is the world's largest lake by surface area (~371,000 km²) and borders five countries: Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan. It contains large reserves of oil and natural gas under its bed
questions.explanation: The Caspian Sea (371,000 km²) is the world's largest enclosed inland body of water — roughly the size of Japan. It is saltwater (though less salty than oceans — about 1.2% vs 3.5% for the ocean). Its five bordering nations debated for decades whether it was a lake (resources divided by area) or a sea (resources divided by maritime law). The Caspian Sea Convention (2018) declared it a special body with its own legal framework — neither sea nor lake. It holds ~50% of the world's caviar-producing sturgeon (although sturgeon populations have severely declined due to overfishing and habitat loss).
questions.memory_hook: The Caspian Sea Convention was signed in 2018 after 20 years of negotiation: is it a sea (maritime law, divide by coastal length) or a lake (divide by area)? They finally agreed it was neither — a special body with its own rules. The stakes were trillions of dollars in oil and gas. It holds 50% of the world's sturgeon, but overfishing has made Caspian caviar a conservation crisis.

questions.question: What is significant about New Zealand's geographic isolation and its effect on its native wildlife?
options: New Zealand's isolation produced large mammal diversity but almost no bird species, the opposite of Australia

New Zealand was isolated for ~80 million years after separating from Gondwana — evolving in the absence of land mammals. Birds filled ecological niches normally occupied by mammals (the kiwi replaced the mammalian forager; the now-extinct moa, up to 3.6m tall, was the top browser). European colonization introduced mammals that devastated endemic bird species
New Zealand's isolation preserved prehistoric reptiles but produced no unique bird species
New Zealand separated from Australia only 2 million years ago; its wildlife is nearly identical to Australian wildlife
questions.answer: New Zealand was isolated for ~80 million years after separating from Gondwana — evolving in the absence of land mammals. Birds filled ecological niches normally occupied by mammals (the kiwi replaced the mammalian forager; the now-extinct moa, up to 3.6m tall, was the top browser). European colonization introduced mammals that devastated endemic bird species
questions.explanation: New Zealand separated from Gondwana ~80 million years ago, before placental mammals diversified. The only native land mammals were bats. With no mammalian predators, birds evolved to fill every niche: the giant moa (herbivore, top browser, 3.6m tall) was hunted to extinction by Polynesian settlers by ~1440 CE. The kiwi became nocturnal, ground-dwelling, and lost flight. The kakapo (world's heaviest parrot) can't fly and was nearly extinct (critically endangered). When humans introduced rats, stoats, and cats, they devastated birds evolved with no defense against mammalian predators.
questions.memory_hook: New Zealand birds had no mammalian predators for 80 million years, so they stopped worrying about the ground. Kiwis lost the ability to fly and became nocturnal ground-foragers. Kakapos became the world's heaviest parrot, too fat to fly. Then Polynesian settlers arrived with rats and dogs; European settlers brought cats, stoats, and weasels. Birds that had survived 80 million years of evolution were gone in decades.

questions.question: What is the function of mitosis versus meiosis in the body?
options: Mitosis produces sex cells (sperm and eggs); meiosis produces all other body cells

Mitosis is cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells — used for growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction in single-celled organisms. Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells (sex cells) with half the chromosome number, enabling genetic recombination through crossing over
Both mitosis and meiosis produce genetically identical cells; the difference is only in the speed of division
Mitosis produces cells in the immune system; meiosis produces all other body cells through the same chromosomal process
questions.answer: Mitosis is cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells — used for growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction in single-celled organisms. Meiosis produces four genetically unique haploid cells (sex cells) with half the chromosome number, enabling genetic recombination through crossing over
questions.explanation: Mitosis: a cell replicates its DNA and divides into two identical daughter cells (2n→2n). Used for growth, wound healing, and replacement of short-lived cells (skin cells live ~2-3 weeks; red blood cells ~120 days; neurons rarely divide). Meiosis: a cell divides twice, producing 4 daughter cells each with half the chromosome number (2n→n). Crossing over (prophase I): homologous chromosomes exchange segments, generating unique genetic combinations. When sperm (n=23) fertilizes egg (n=23), the result is a zygote with the full 46 chromosomes. Error in meiosis: non-disjunction (chromosomes fail to separate) → Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Turner syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome.
questions.memory_hook: Meiosis has an extra step that mitosis doesn't: crossing over, where homologous chromosomes swap segments before separating. This shuffling creates genetic diversity — each sex cell has a unique combination of genes from both parents. Without crossing over, siblings would be far more genetically identical than they are. It's the engine of genetic diversity, running every time anyone has children.

questions.question: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web, and what happens to an ecosystem when a 'keystone species' is removed?
options: A food chain and food web are synonymous; removing any species has proportional effects on the ecosystem

A food chain is a linear sequence of 'who eats whom'; a food web shows the full network of feeding relationships. A keystone species has disproportionate impact relative to its abundance — removing it causes cascading collapse of the ecosystem. Example: sea otters eat sea urchins; without otters, urchins overgraze kelp forests, destroying the ecosystem
A food web is more specific than a food chain — it tracks a single species' diet across a year rather than a single predator-prey link
Keystone species are always apex predators; removing small organisms never significantly disrupts ecosystems
questions.answer: A food chain is a linear sequence of 'who eats whom'; a food web shows the full network of feeding relationships. A keystone species has disproportionate impact relative to its abundance — removing it causes cascading collapse of the ecosystem. Example: sea otters eat sea urchins; without otters, urchins overgraze kelp forests, destroying the ecosystem
questions.explanation: The keystone species concept was defined by ecologist Robert Paine in the 1960s via sea star (starfish) removal experiments: removing Pisaster ochraceus from a tidal pool allowed mussels to take over, eliminating 15+ other species. The wolf reintroduction to Yellowstone (1995) triggered a 'trophic cascade': wolves reduced elk overgrazing → willows and aspens recovered → beavers returned → rivers changed course. This is called 'the ecology of fear' — prey animals avoid risky grazing areas even if predators are sparse, changing vegetation patterns. Food web: most organisms participate in multiple chains simultaneously, creating network resilience.
questions.memory_hook: Sea otters are a keystone species: they eat sea urchins, which eat kelp. Without otters, urchins graze kelp forests to bare rock, taking hundreds of species with them. When wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone, elk stopped grazing near rivers (fear of wolves), willows grew back, beavers returned, and rivers changed course. A predator changed the shape of rivers by changing where elk stood. That's a trophic cascade.
